content.text:
Got it. The scene-setting first beat is dead weight. Both beats should teach. Let me make beat 1 carry the actual concept (the problem it solves) and beat 2 carry the actions.

**Option A**
1. `You have more to fix than time to fix it. Subagents do the work for you, several at once.`
2. `Drag one onto a track to send it off. When it finishes, run entire checkpoint explain to see what it did.`

**Option B** (value-forward)
1. `Subagents do your work in parallel, so five jobs take about the time of one.`
2. `Drag one onto a track to send it off, then run entire checkpoint explain to see what it did.`

**Option C** (problem as a question)
1. `Too much to fix alone? Subagents take the work and run it all at once.`
2. `Drag them onto the tracks to send them, then check the result with entire checkpoint explain.`

Every line now does a job: beat 1 = the problem + why subagents matter (parallel = fast), beat 2 = the two verbs (send, then `explain`). Drag moves into beat 2 here instead of a live nudge, since you wanted both beats to pull weight.

B is the tightest and most concept-true (it states the parallel payoff as a number-ish fact). A spells the problem out more plainly. Which one, or want me to blend?

message.content: i fixed the view a little bit but it's still not great..i think i need the island to be bigger..i wanna feel like im on the island for level 2..i feel like i cant really see the beams or the hazards. i feel like there needs to be even more tracks but maybe less cars and maybe the cars can come back and reuse some of them..u get me..like maybe i have like 12 tracks?? and then just 6 cars..idk so theres two things the view/visibility and then amping up the multitasking with more tracks

content.text:
Two separate problems, and they actually pull on each other, so let me take them together.

## 1. Visibility / "on the island"

The root cause: one fixed camera has to frame the whole strip from the bay (front) to the systems (way back), so everything is far and tiny. And there's a geometry conflict baked in:

- **Beams are vertical.** They read best from a *low, near-horizontal* camera (tall columns of light). Our current high angle squashes them.
- **Hazards are flat discs on the ground.** Those read best from *above*. A low camera turns them into thin ellipses you can barely see.

So "lower the camera to feel on the island" actively *hurts* the flat hazards. The fix is to stop fighting it: drop the camera lower and closer for presence, **make the hazards volumetric** (a crackling dome / rising corruption field instead of a floor decal) so they read at a low angle, and make the beams taller/brighter. "Bigger island" is really a camera-distance feeling more than terrain size (the terrain's already 200 wide; we just see a sliver) so we can also sprinkle props near the tracks to sell presence.

But here's the thing: **if you also want way more tracks, no single fixed angle can show 12 tracks readably.** That forces the real answer:

**A light mission-control camera you can pan and zoom** (drag to look, scroll to zoom). Lean in to watch a car fight through a hazard, pull back to see the whole board. This is *not* the first-person walking you killed in v1, it's an RTS-style eye over a fixed scene. I think the more-tracks ambition basically requires it. Are you good with that, or do you want to stay strictly no-controls?

## 2. More tracks, fewer cars, reuse

I like it, it amps the dispatch fantasy into a real throughput loop: a pool of cars smaller than the jobs, so you send → they finish and drive home → you re-send to the next open track, racing the clock. Cars become a scarce resource you allocate (and nitro helps a bogged one). That's much more multitasking than 5-cars-5-lanes-once.

Two collisions to decide before I build it:

**a) The review phase doesn't scale.** Right now every job makes you run `explain` and ADD TO SHIP, which is what makes reading non-skippable. Do that 12 times and it's a slog. Two ways out:
   - **Keep a few "hero" jobs** (the 5 curated systems with rich explain cards) that you must review, and the *extra* tracks are quick generic repairs that just need a car, no deep review.
   - **Shift the model**: review becomes a *spot-check* (open `explain` on any car you're curious about, optional), and one `entire dispatch` at the end summarizes *all* of it. This actually mirrors reality better (you don't read every agent's work, you sample and let the summary roll it up) and it's what unlocks 10-12 tracks without tedium. Cost: `explain` stops being a forced gate.

**b) Level 3 quizzes the 5 curated systems.** More jobs means inventing more than 5. Cleanest: keep the 5 hero systems for story + L3, add generic "minor repair" tracks on top for the throughput.

My lean: light pan/zoom camera + volumetric hazards, ~6 cars over ~9 tracks (5 hero + 4 generic), cars auto-return after sealing, and the **spot-check + dispatch-summarizes-all** review model. That gets you the busy, can't-do-it-alone feeling and keeps the end clean.

Before I touch code: (1) camera control yes/no, and (2) review as forced-gate-on-a-few vs spot-check-plus-summary? 

(Also parking the onboarding copy from before, we'll lock that after this.)

message.content: no leave level 3 alone.and why doesnt the review phase scale. it's a friction point on purpose.

content.text:
Fair — I was wrong to call that a problem. You're right: the review *is* the friction, and friction is the lesson. Looking at what an agent did before you accept it is the whole point of `explain`, and having that cost scale with how much you delegated is actually good design, not a flaw. Delegate six jobs, you owe six reviews. You should *feel* the oversight. I'll drop the "spot-check + summarize" idea entirely and keep `explain` + ADD TO SHIP as a hard gate on every job.

And L3 stays frozen: the five curated systems keep their checkpoints and cards exactly as they are.

So here's the shape with your constraints locked in:

- **The 5 hero systems stay** (story + L3, untouched).
- **More tracks = add generic repair jobs** on top of those five. They get their own simpler flavor cards to read, so the review friction applies to them too. L3 never sees them.
- **Fewer cars than tracks** → you're forced to reuse. A car seals its job, drives back to the bay on its own, and you re-dispatch it to the next open track. Cars become the scarce resource you're juggling.

That gives a real loop: dispatch a wave → rescue the bogged ones with nitro → review + accept each finish (the friction) → cars come home → re-send them → repeat until every job's done → final `dispatch`. Much busier, genuinely multitasking.

One thing to decide on reuse: a car can drive home and get re-sent **the moment it seals**, *before* you've reviewed that job — so a review backlog can pile up while you keep dispatching. That backlog under the clock is where the multitasking pressure really comes from. Good with that?

Open calls before I build:

1. **Counts.** Your 12/6 is a lot of reading. I'd start at **8 tracks (5 hero + 3 generic) / 4 cars** and we tune up. Want to start smaller or go straight for 12/6?
2. **Camera** (still unanswered, and it gates whether 8-12 tracks are even *visible*): light pan/zoom mission-control eye, yes or no? With this many tracks I don't think a single fixed angle can show them readably — but I'll also make hazards volumetric and beams taller either way so you can actually see them.

message.content: so are you okay with entire checkpoint explain still being required

content.text:
Yeah, I'm genuinely fine with it being required. Required is correct here, and for a reason I should've led with instead of arguing scale: if `explain` is optional, players skip it, and the one thing this level exists to teach is gone. "You delegated, so you owe a look before you accept" only lands if the look is mandatory. So keep the hard gate.

My actual worry was never "required is bad" — it was "required times twelve turns reading into stamping." Those are different things. The first two or three reviews, you read because the card is fresh and you're curious what the agent improvised. By the ninth identical-feeling job you're mashing Enter, Y, Enter, Y without your eyes on the text. The gate is still there, but the *comprehension* it's supposed to force has quietly left. That's the failure mode I want to avoid — not the requirement, the dilution.

And there's a constraint that makes this sharper: you've ruled out judging the work (no reject mechanic, no botched-agent puzzles). So reading is never decision-relevant — you always accept. That's totally fine at a handful of jobs, where you read because it's interesting. But it means the only thing keeping reviews from going on autopilot is **how many there are** and **how worth-reading each card is.** The requirement can't carry that on its own.

So: keep `explain` required, yes. Just treat the job count as the real dial. I think the friction stays *live* at around 5-6 reviews and starts going *ceremonial* north of that. You can still get the reuse/throughput juggle from cars being scarcer than jobs without going all the way to 12 — e.g. 4 cars over 6-7 jobs already forces constant re-dispatching.

Does that distinction land for you — required, but dosage matters — or do you actually want all twelve required and reading-fatigue be damned?

Here's what's locked:

- **12 tracks**: the 5 hero systems (frozen, L3 untouched) + 7 generic repair jobs, each with its own card to read. All 12 gated by `explain` + ADD TO SHIP.
- **6 cars**, reused. A car seals its job, drives itself back to the bay, and you re-dispatch it. Review backlog allowed — sealed jobs wait for you while cars keep rolling.
- Hazards + nitro carry over.

The one thing I still need from you, and it's now basically forced by the twelve: **camera.** You can't fit 12 readable tracks in one fixed frame — they'd be tiny, which is the exact visibility complaint that started this. So I want to add a **light pan/zoom mission-control camera** (drag to look, scroll to zoom; not the first-person walking you killed). Pull back for the whole board, lean in to watch a car fight a hazard or to read a beam. Plus volumetric hazards and taller beams so they actually show.

That's my plan. Only flag if you *don't* want camera control — otherwise I'll build it with pan/zoom. Good to go?

input.new_string:
Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide: LANE(i,n) startX (i-5.5)*9 finishX *14 (±77 within terrain ±100). NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: level2-drone-bay-design
description: "Agreed L2 (2026-06-10) — 'The Drone Bay' teaches `entire checkpoint explain`; Archive/search level shelved for later"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
---

Agreed with Rizel 2026-06-10, replacing the built Archive level (shelved, kept in `src/archiveView.js`, playable at `?view=archive`). BUILT 2026-06-10 in `src/droneBayView.js` (#db-hud markup, post-L1 pin + ?view=level2 route here now); 150s clock, 18s drone work time, both tunable. Drones IMPROVISE (Rizel's "bow turns into a sword"): coils→Plasma Ring, nav core→Star Dome, dish→Signal Spire, scrubbers→Garden Pod, struts→Grav Skid — the transformation creates the "what did you DO?" urge that explain satisfies. Beam-color status grammar: amber broken → cyan working → ice-blue review-me → none (upgrade is the landmark). Ground levels in the day-to-day agentic workflow: **delegate → review → accept**. Level 2 "The Drone Bay": 5 broken ship systems (ignition coils, nav core, antenna, life support, landing struts), 5 drones (one per job, no economy). Assign with E (drone flies out and works); player chooses serial vs. parallel — the one countdown makes parallel the rational move. Finished sites seal under a checkpoint; walk up → terminal opens **pre-filled** with `entire checkpoint explain <id>` (Rizel: never type ids) → Enter → story card of what the drone did → **ADD TO SHIP** confirm (Y) → meter +20%. Finish line (changed 2026-06-10): the FIFTH accept keeps the terminal open and you type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report writes itself from the checkpoints ("Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" per the real CLI). Rizel couldn't find the end-of-level console ("where am I supposed to be going") → no final fetch-quest; next command at the prompt you're already at. `entire checkpoint list` still accepted as an optional L1 callback. Bird's-eye map (M, src/overhead.js) added to all island levels; terminals stay open over it. Deliberately excluded: botched-drone deduction puzzles, drone management, extracting values from explanations (Rizel rejected those as "too complicated"). Teaching — use Rizel's framing (2026-06-10), not workflow jargon like "delegate→review→accept": the level shows **multiple agents working in parallel, seeing what work each did (explain), and getting a summary at the end (dispatch)**. Sending a drone is one button (spectacle, not a lesson); ADD TO SHIP is just the gate that makes reading the explanation non-skippable. Terminology (Rizel, 2026-06-10): in all player-facing copy the workers are called **subagents**, never drones — only the location keeps the name "The Drone Bay" (possible later rename; candidates discussed: The Fleet, Mission Control, fiction-patch "drones piloted by subagents"). Why: the word should make the real-world mapping unmistakable — these are agents working in parallel (or sequentially, player's choice) and you get insight into their work. Not metaphor decoration. See [[level2-archive-search-design]] and [[level2-design-direction]].

**REWORKED 2026-06-16 — starting-grid / mission-control rebuild.** Rizel disliked walking the island to scattered sites; redesigned the dispatch layer (NOT the explain/dispatch teaching, which is unchanged) into a fixed-camera racing-grid. droneBayView.js rewritten: dropped first-person (firstPerson.js), overhead map (overhead.js), pointer-lock, walk-up proximity, and the E-assign key. Now: fixed camera over 5 parallel lanes; subagents are **cars** (not drones) parked on a grid, rails drawn along terrain. **Click a car = arm; Space = green flag (launches all armed at once); click a moving car = nitro boost.** This one mechanic gives both sequential (arm-one-then-Space) and parallel (arm all five) — Rizel explicitly wanted both. Nitro = the engagement-during-travel + catch-up tool (3 charges/run, shown as dots in #db-systems-rows); Rizel didn't care what "powerups" meant, "just something where they go faster." Lanes are LOCAL to L2 (props placed at lane-ends, START_Z=64→FINISH_Z=-34, slight fan) and decoupled from SYSTEMS.pos — left SYSTEMS.pos untouched because launchView.js (L3) still reads it for its own island. Tunables: BASE_SPEED 1/14, BOOST_MULT 2.6, BOOST_DUR 2.2, NITRO_MAX 3, TOTAL_TIME 150. Kept: clock+panic sky, checkpoint cards, click-sealed-car→pre-filled explain→ADD TO SHIP→dispatch finish, win/fail. Open/untested live: car speed feel, whether any lane-end finish lands in water. Briefing + CONTROLS in index.html (#db-briefing) rewritten to match (click/Space, no WASD/E/M).

**TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

aiTitle: Improve level 2 navigation and subagent workflow

content.text:
Built and clean. Level 2 is now a dispatch yard. Reload and have at it.

**What changed:**

- **12 tracks, 6 cars, reuse.** Five hero systems + seven generic repair jobs, all gated by `explain` + ADD TO SHIP (every one of the twelve, as you called it). A car seals its job, drives itself back to the bay, and you re-dispatch it. Cars are the scarce thing you're juggling.
- **Review by clicking the finished job.** Since the car drives off after sealing, you now click the ice-blue *job* at the end of the track to review it. The review queue stacks up while cars keep rolling — that's the multitasking pressure.
- **Free camera.** Drag the background to orbit, scroll to zoom. Pull back for the whole yard, lean in to watch a car fight a patch. (Dragging a *car* still drags the car; dragging empty space moves the camera.)
- **Visibility fixes.** Hazards are now volumetric (glowing shard clusters with a dome, not flat decals) so they read from a low angle, and the beams are taller and brighter.
- **Aggregate status panel.** Twelve named rows wouldn't fit, so the side panel now summarizes: systems online x/12, en route / to review / bogged, and cars free.
- Clock starts on the first green flag, stops once all twelve are sealed. Reviewing stays untimed.

**Untuned, flag what feels off:** `TOTAL_TIME 80`, `NITRO_MAX 6`, 6 cars, ~4 severe patches. With 12 reviews you'll get the first real read on whether the friction holds or turns into stamping — that was the open question, now you'll actually feel it.

Two things still open from before, parked but not forgotten:
1. **Onboarding copy** — you were mid-pick between the 2-beat options (A/B/C). Current build still has the old 3-beat version.
2. The dead `#db-briefing-start` CSS.

Want to lock the onboarding copy next, or play this first?

I need to compress the whole yard onto land and drop the camera. Let me verify a compact layout against the real terrain before committing.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: level2-drone-bay-design
description: "Agreed L2 (2026-06-10) — 'The Drone Bay' teaches `entire checkpoint explain`; Archive/search level shelved for later"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
---

Agreed with Rizel 2026-06-10, replacing the built Archive level (shelved, kept in `src/archiveView.js`, playable at `?view=archive`). BUILT 2026-06-10 in `src/droneBayView.js` (#db-hud markup, post-L1 pin + ?view=level2 route here now); 150s clock, 18s drone work time, both tunable. Drones IMPROVISE (Rizel's "bow turns into a sword"): coils→Plasma Ring, nav core→Star Dome, dish→Signal Spire, scrubbers→Garden Pod, struts→Grav Skid — the transformation creates the "what did you DO?" urge that explain satisfies. Beam-color status grammar: amber broken → cyan working → ice-blue review-me → none (upgrade is the landmark). Ground levels in the day-to-day agentic workflow: **delegate → review → accept**. Level 2 "The Drone Bay": 5 broken ship systems (ignition coils, nav core, antenna, life support, landing struts), 5 drones (one per job, no economy). Assign with E (drone flies out and works); player chooses serial vs. parallel — the one countdown makes parallel the rational move. Finished sites seal under a checkpoint; walk up → terminal opens **pre-filled** with `entire checkpoint explain <id>` (Rizel: never type ids) → Enter → story card of what the drone did → **ADD TO SHIP** confirm (Y) → meter +20%. Finish line (changed 2026-06-10): the FIFTH accept keeps the terminal open and you type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report writes itself from the checkpoints ("Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" per the real CLI). Rizel couldn't find the end-of-level console ("where am I supposed to be going") → no final fetch-quest; next command at the prompt you're already at. `entire checkpoint list` still accepted as an optional L1 callback. Bird's-eye map (M, src/overhead.js) added to all island levels; terminals stay open over it. Deliberately excluded: botched-drone deduction puzzles, drone management, extracting values from explanations (Rizel rejected those as "too complicated"). Teaching — use Rizel's framing (2026-06-10), not workflow jargon like "delegate→review→accept": the level shows **multiple agents working in parallel, seeing what work each did (explain), and getting a summary at the end (dispatch)**. Sending a drone is one button (spectacle, not a lesson); ADD TO SHIP is just the gate that makes reading the explanation non-skippable. Terminology (Rizel, 2026-06-10): in all player-facing copy the workers are called **subagents**, never drones — only the location keeps the name "The Drone Bay" (possible later rename; candidates discussed: The Fleet, Mission Control, fiction-patch "drones piloted by subagents"). Why: the word should make the real-world mapping unmistakable — these are agents working in parallel (or sequentially, player's choice) and you get insight into their work. Not metaphor decoration. See [[level2-archive-search-design]] and [[level2-design-direction]].

**REWORKED 2026-06-16 — starting-grid / mission-control rebuild.** Rizel disliked walking the island to scattered sites; redesigned the dispatch layer (NOT the explain/dispatch teaching, which is unchanged) into a fixed-camera racing-grid. droneBayView.js rewritten: dropped first-person (firstPerson.js), overhead map (overhead.js), pointer-lock, walk-up proximity, and the E-assign key. Now: fixed camera over 5 parallel lanes; subagents are **cars** (not drones) parked on a grid, rails drawn along terrain. **Click a car = arm; Space = green flag (launches all armed at once); click a moving car = nitro boost.** This one mechanic gives both sequential (arm-one-then-Space) and parallel (arm all five) — Rizel explicitly wanted both. Nitro = the engagement-during-travel + catch-up tool (3 charges/run, shown as dots in #db-systems-rows); Rizel didn't care what "powerups" meant, "just something where they go faster." Lanes are LOCAL to L2 (props placed at lane-ends, START_Z=64→FINISH_Z=-34, slight fan) and decoupled from SYSTEMS.pos — left SYSTEMS.pos untouched because launchView.js (L3) still reads it for its own island. Tunables: BASE_SPEED 1/14, BOOST_MULT 2.6, BOOST_DUR 2.2, NITRO_MAX 3, TOTAL_TIME 150. Kept: clock+panic sky, checkpoint cards, click-sealed-car→pre-filled explain→ADD TO SHIP→dispatch finish, win/fail. Open/untested live: car speed feel, whether any lane-end finish lands in water. Briefing + CONTROLS in index.html (#db-briefing) rewritten to match (click/Space, no WASD/E/M).

**TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide: LANE(i,n) startX (i-5.5)*9 finishX *14 (±77 within terrain ±100). NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

content.text:
Done. Reload and look again.

**Fixes:**

- **No more water.** The yard was spread so wide the outer tracks ran off the island into the sea (land only reliably exists within ~radius 60 of center; finishes were at ~84). I compacted the whole thing onto a centered land patch and **verified it against the actual terrain** — sampled `heightAt` across every track, start, finish, and bay slot; the lowest point is 1.54 above the waterline. Nothing drives through water now.
- **Less bird's-eye.** Camera dropped lower and pulled in (height ~21 instead of ~40, closer in), and you can zoom in tighter than before. It reads as standing near the yard, not floating over the island.
- **A real spot for the cars.** Added a glowing depot outline on the ground around the bay so the cars have a marked home that hugs the terrain (no clipping/floating).

The tradeoff: shorter tracks now (the island patch is smaller), so traversal is quicker — that may want a clock retune once you play it.

One thing I want to flag honestly: you opened with "I don't know if I like this." I fixed the concrete complaints (water, camera, the cars' spot), but if the **12-track scale itself** is what's not landing, that's a bigger call than placement — say the word and we can dial it back. Want to play this pass first, or talk scale?

content.text: Builds clean. Let me correct the memory numbers so they're not stale, then summarize.

input.old_string: Compacted onto a centred land patch — START_Z=30, FINISH_Z=-28, BAY_Z=46, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *8, baySlot *7 — verified on-land with a node script sampling terrain.heightAt across the whole footprint (min height 1.54 > 0). Camera lowered/closer: camTarget(0,1,6), camRadius 86 (zoom 40-170), camPhi 1.3 — less top-down. Added a depot outline (terrainRectLine, terrain-hugging LineLoop) around the bay = the cars' "spot."
input.new_string: Compacted onto a centred land patch. Iterated again (Rizel: "big island mostly what I see, no water, beams not in water, a DOCK with the cars, view starts on the tracks"): current layout START_Z=34, FINISH_Z=-24, BAY_Z=48, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *7, baySlot *7 — verified with node script /tmp/landcheck.mjs sampling terrain.heightAt: min finish/beam height 4.24 (solid gold land, not shore), min track 1.02, bay 3.99. Built a real DOCK: one flat deckY (max terrain across bay +0.5), deck box on 4 stilt posts + glowing cyan edge, cars parked ON the deck (home.y=deckY+0.35). Camera lowered/closer: camTarget(0,2,5), camRadius 88 (zoom 44-170), camPhi 1.24, clamps theta ±0.8 / phi 0.72-1.36 (phi capped so orbiting can't tilt down to the waterline). KEY TRICK for "no water": tightened fog to 80-190 so the distant sea hazes into the sky colour instead of trying to win the geometry fight (you can't both hide water AND avoid bird's-eye on a round island — fog resolves it). Tracks are now SHORT (z 34→-24 ≈ 58u) so traversal is quick — clock/ speed likely need a retune. Onboarding copy STILL pending (2-beat A/B/C).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: level2-drone-bay-design
description: "Agreed L2 (2026-06-10) — 'The Drone Bay' teaches `entire checkpoint explain`; Archive/search level shelved for later"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
---

Agreed with Rizel 2026-06-10, replacing the built Archive level (shelved, kept in `src/archiveView.js`, playable at `?view=archive`). BUILT 2026-06-10 in `src/droneBayView.js` (#db-hud markup, post-L1 pin + ?view=level2 route here now); 150s clock, 18s drone work time, both tunable. Drones IMPROVISE (Rizel's "bow turns into a sword"): coils→Plasma Ring, nav core→Star Dome, dish→Signal Spire, scrubbers→Garden Pod, struts→Grav Skid — the transformation creates the "what did you DO?" urge that explain satisfies. Beam-color status grammar: amber broken → cyan working → ice-blue review-me → none (upgrade is the landmark). Ground levels in the day-to-day agentic workflow: **delegate → review → accept**. Level 2 "The Drone Bay": 5 broken ship systems (ignition coils, nav core, antenna, life support, landing struts), 5 drones (one per job, no economy). Assign with E (drone flies out and works); player chooses serial vs. parallel — the one countdown makes parallel the rational move. Finished sites seal under a checkpoint; walk up → terminal opens **pre-filled** with `entire checkpoint explain <id>` (Rizel: never type ids) → Enter → story card of what the drone did → **ADD TO SHIP** confirm (Y) → meter +20%. Finish line (changed 2026-06-10): the FIFTH accept keeps the terminal open and you type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report writes itself from the checkpoints ("Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" per the real CLI). Rizel couldn't find the end-of-level console ("where am I supposed to be going") → no final fetch-quest; next command at the prompt you're already at. `entire checkpoint list` still accepted as an optional L1 callback. Bird's-eye map (M, src/overhead.js) added to all island levels; terminals stay open over it. Deliberately excluded: botched-drone deduction puzzles, drone management, extracting values from explanations (Rizel rejected those as "too complicated"). Teaching — use Rizel's framing (2026-06-10), not workflow jargon like "delegate→review→accept": the level shows **multiple agents working in parallel, seeing what work each did (explain), and getting a summary at the end (dispatch)**. Sending a drone is one button (spectacle, not a lesson); ADD TO SHIP is just the gate that makes reading the explanation non-skippable. Terminology (Rizel, 2026-06-10): in all player-facing copy the workers are called **subagents**, never drones — only the location keeps the name "The Drone Bay" (possible later rename; candidates discussed: The Fleet, Mission Control, fiction-patch "drones piloted by subagents"). Why: the word should make the real-world mapping unmistakable — these are agents working in parallel (or sequentially, player's choice) and you get insight into their work. Not metaphor decoration. See [[level2-archive-search-design]] and [[level2-design-direction]].

**REWORKED 2026-06-16 — starting-grid / mission-control rebuild.** Rizel disliked walking the island to scattered sites; redesigned the dispatch layer (NOT the explain/dispatch teaching, which is unchanged) into a fixed-camera racing-grid. droneBayView.js rewritten: dropped first-person (firstPerson.js), overhead map (overhead.js), pointer-lock, walk-up proximity, and the E-assign key. Now: fixed camera over 5 parallel lanes; subagents are **cars** (not drones) parked on a grid, rails drawn along terrain. **Click a car = arm; Space = green flag (launches all armed at once); click a moving car = nitro boost.** This one mechanic gives both sequential (arm-one-then-Space) and parallel (arm all five) — Rizel explicitly wanted both. Nitro = the engagement-during-travel + catch-up tool (3 charges/run, shown as dots in #db-systems-rows); Rizel didn't care what "powerups" meant, "just something where they go faster." Lanes are LOCAL to L2 (props placed at lane-ends, START_Z=64→FINISH_Z=-34, slight fan) and decoupled from SYSTEMS.pos — left SYSTEMS.pos untouched because launchView.js (L3) still reads it for its own island. Tunables: BASE_SPEED 1/14, BOOST_MULT 2.6, BOOST_DUR 2.2, NITRO_MAX 3, TOTAL_TIME 150. Kept: clock+panic sky, checkpoint cards, click-sealed-car→pre-filled explain→ADD TO SHIP→dispatch finish, win/fail. Open/untested live: car speed feel, whether any lane-end finish lands in water. Briefing + CONTROLS in index.html (#db-briefing) rewritten to match (click/Space, no WASD/E/M).

**TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide. **WATER/CAMERA FIX 2026-06-16** (Rizel: too bird's-eye, cars in the water, wants a spot on the island): terrain seaLevel=0 and land is only reliable within ~radius 60 of centre, so the original wide spread (finishX ±77) put cars in the sea. Compacted onto a centred land patch — START_Z=30, FINISH_Z=-28, BAY_Z=46, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *8, baySlot *7 — verified on-land with a node script sampling terrain.heightAt across the whole footprint (min height 1.54 > 0). Camera lowered/closer: camTarget(0,1,6), camRadius 86 (zoom 40-170), camPhi 1.3 — less top-down. Added a depot outline (terrainRectLine, terrain-hugging LineLoop) around the bay = the cars' "spot." NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

content.text:
Done. Reload and check the framing.

**What changed:**

- **Beams out of the water.** Systems pulled inland — every beam now sits at min height ~4.2 (solid gold land), confirmed by sampling the real terrain, not guessing. No beam rises from the shallows anymore.
- **A real dock.** The cars now park on a built platform — a deck on stilts with a glowing edge — instead of just sitting on the dirt. They have a literal place.
- **Island fills the view, water gone.** Here's the honest bit: you can't *both* hide all water *and* avoid a bird's-eye angle on a round island — tilt down to hide the sea and it goes top-down; tilt up to feel grounded and the sea comes into frame. So I solved it with **fog** instead of geometry: tightened the haze (80–190) so the distant sea dissolves into the sky color. You get a low, grounded angle AND the water reads as atmosphere, not ocean. I also capped how far you can tilt the camera down when orbiting, so you can't swing it around and find the waterline.
- **Starts on the tracks.** Camera now opens framed on the track corridor — dock and cars in the foreground, tracks running out to the systems.

**Heads up:** the playable patch is smaller now, so tracks are short and cars finish fast. The clock (`TOTAL_TIME 80`) and speeds will probably want a retune once you feel it — tell me if it's too easy/slow and I'll tighten.

Take a look — if the island still doesn't read as "big," the lever is zooming the camera in closer by default (smaller `camRadius`), which I can do in one line.

lines: **TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

-**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide. **WATER/CAMERA FIX 2026-06-16** (Rizel: too bird's-eye, cars in the water, wants a spot on the island): terrain seaLevel=0 and land is only reliable within ~radius 60 of centre, so the original wide spread (finishX ±77) put cars in the sea. Compacted onto a centred land patch. Iterated again (Rizel: "big island mostly what I see, no water, beams not in water, a DOCK with the cars, view starts on the tracks"): current layout START_Z=34, FINISH_Z=-24, BAY_Z=48, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *7, baySlot *7 — verified with node script /tmp/landcheck.mjs sampling terrain.heightAt: min finish/beam height 4.24 (solid gold land, not shore), min track 1.02, bay 3.99. Built a real DOCK: one flat deckY (max terrain across bay +0.5), deck box on 4 stilt posts + glowing cyan edge, cars parked ON the deck (home.y=deckY+0.35). Camera lowered/closer: camTarget(0,2,5), camRadius 88 (zoom 44-170), camPhi 1.24, clamps theta ±0.8 / phi 0.72-1.36 (phi capped so orbiting can't tilt down to the waterline). KEY TRICK for "no water": tightened fog to 80-190 so the distant sea hazes into the sky colour instead of trying to win the geometry fight (you can't both hide water AND avoid bird's-eye on a round island — fog resolves it). Tracks are now SHORT (z 34→-24 ≈ 58u) so traversal is quick — clock/ speed likely need a retune. Onboarding copy STILL pending (2-beat A/B/C). NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

content.text:
Done. Reload and look.

**Both changes:**

- **Dock is off to the side.** It's now a pier running down the right (+x) edge of the island, posts and all. The six cars wait in a column on it, facing left toward the track field, and you drag them left onto the track starts. The center of the view is clear for the tracks. (Drop-detection is now distance-based, so dropping a car on a lane works regardless of where the dock sits.)
- **Camera opens on the tracks.** Default view is pulled in closer (radius 88 → 66) and aimed straight down the track corridor at `(0, 1.5, 4)`, theta 0 — so you start looking *along* the twelve tracks toward the systems, with the dock sitting at the right edge of the frame rather than filling the foreground. You can still orbit/zoom from there.

If the dock should be on the **left** instead of the right, that's a one-line flip (`DOCK_X = -46`). And if the camera should sit even tighter on the tracks, I can drop `camRadius` further or nudge the target. Tell me what it looks like.

lines: **TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

-**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide. **WATER/CAMERA FIX 2026-06-16** (Rizel: too bird's-eye, cars in the water, wants a spot on the island): terrain seaLevel=0 and land is only reliable within ~radius 60 of centre, so the original wide spread (finishX ±77) put cars in the sea. Compacted onto a centred land patch. Iterated again (Rizel: "big island mostly what I see, no water, beams not in water, a DOCK with the cars, view starts on the tracks"): current layout START_Z=34, FINISH_Z=-24, BAY_Z=48, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *7, baySlot *7 — verified with node script /tmp/landcheck.mjs sampling terrain.heightAt: min finish/beam height 4.24 (solid gold land, not shore), min track 1.02, bay 3.99. Built a real DOCK, then moved it OFF TO THE SIDE (Rizel: dock to the side, view should start on the tracks). Dock is now a pier on the +x edge: DOCK_X=46, cars in a z-column (DOCK_Z0=24, DOCK_DZ=-6, 6 slots z 24..-6), baySlot(i)={x:46,z:24-6i}, deck box (narrow x=10, long z) on 4 stilts + cyan rim, cars parked ON deck (home.y=deckY+0.35) facing -x (BAY_FACING=π/2, nose→-x toward the field). nearestEmptyLane now distance-to-start-point (hypot<9), geometry-agnostic so side-dock drops work. Tracks stay centred (startX*6 finishX*7). Camera reframed tighter ON the tracks: camTarget(0,1.5,4), camRadius 66 (zoom 36-150), camPhi 1.22, theta 0 so it opens looking straight down the track corridor with the dock at the right edge; clamps theta ±0.8 / phi 0.72-1.36 (phi capped so orbiting can't reach the waterline). KEY TRICK for "no water": tightened fog to 80-190 so the distant sea hazes into the sky colour instead of trying to win the geometry fight (you can't both hide water AND avoid bird's-eye on a round island — fog resolves it). Tracks are now SHORT (z 34→-24 ≈ 58u) so traversal is quick — clock/ speed likely need a retune. Onboarding copy STILL pending (2-beat A/B/C). NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

content.text:
Done. Reload and see.

**What changed:**

- **Tracks now start at the front of the island.** The whole field is shifted forward toward the near shore (start line moved from z=34 to z=50) and the fan is narrower so the outer lanes don't run off into the water. Tracks begin up front and run inward to the systems on the high central ground. Verified on the real terrain — lowest point anywhere is 2.2, beams at 3.3+ (solid land).
- **Zoomed in.** Camera pulled from radius 66 to 52 and aimed at the start of the tracks (z=24), so you open looking down the track corridor from its beginning, dock at the right.

**The honest constraint I keep hitting, so you know the lever:** the **12-lane width** is what fights both of these. The island's good land is a central dome; the front edge is low shore. A wide fan pushed forward hits water, and a wide fan zoomed in pushes the outer lanes off-screen. I narrowed the fan to buy room, but it's now fairly tight (lanes 4 units apart at the start). If you want it pushed even further forward or zoomed even tighter, the clean way is **fewer lanes** — that's the trade. You locked 12 earlier, so I'm not changing it without your say-so; just flagging that's the dial if this still isn't close enough.

Take a look and tell me what's off.
